one_line_takeaway: Enforcing Lipschitz continuity (or PGD adversarial training) on neural Lyapunov-Barrier certificates, verified in a CEGIS loop with Marabou, yields deterministic robustness guarantees for RL controllers, improving certified perturbation bounds up to 4.6x and empirical success rates up to 2.4x over a non-robust baseline on two control tasks.
key_finding: All three robust-training methods beat the non-robust Vanilla baseline on both certified and empirical robustness. Certified perturbation bound (Table 1): on Inverted Pendulum, Lip-3 raises it from 0.0015 to 0.0084 — a 460% (~4.6x) gain; on 2D Docking, Lip-1 raises it from 0.0094 to 0.0172 (+83%). Because bounds are l∞, the authors note certified volume grows >30x (2D pendulum state) and >10x (4D docking state). Empirical success rate (Table 2, over 10,000 sampled initial states, success = reach goal within 200 steps without hitting unsafe set): on Inverted Pendulum at 0.01 adversarial perturbation, Vanilla=39% vs Lip-3=70%, Lip-Neighbor=69%, PGD=89%; on 2D Docking, Lip-1/Lip-Neighbor hold 100% up to 0.03 (2.4x Vanilla's 41%) and retain 83%/86% at 0.05. A key nuance the paper itself highlights: PGD generalizes poorly beyond its trained radius (2D Docking at 0.05: PGD drops to 17%, below even Vanilla's 34%), whereas Lipschitz-based methods degrade more gracefully by enforcing a smaller/smoother Lipschitz constant.
core_contribution: The paper studies, for the first time (per the authors' own contribution list), the synthesis of neural Lyapunov-Barrier certificates for deep-RL controllers that provably retain their safety and stability (reach-while-avoid) guarantees under norm-bounded, time-varying perturbations in the system dynamics/states. Prior neural-certificate work (e.g., FOSSIL [1], Neural Lyapunov Control [6], Mandal et al. [22]) only guarantees validity under fixed ideal dynamics; the authors explicitly distinguish their deterministic robustness guarantee from prior stochastic/probabilistic reach-avoid work ([17,44]). Their own contributions are: (1) a formal Robust Control Lyapunov Barrier Function (CLBF) condition (Def. 4.2) that strengthens the classical descent condition to require V to decrease across an entire lp-ball neighborhood of the perturbed next state, with a robust RWA guarantee theorem (Thm 4.1); (2) Lipschitz-continuity-based sufficient conditions linking the certificate's global Lipschitz constant Lp to admissible perturbation radius δ (Thms 4.2, 4.3: robust condition holds with margin ϵr − Lp·δ); and (3) three practical training losses enforcing this — adversarial (PGD) descent loss, a Lipschitz-neighborhood bound loss, and global Lipschitz regularization (spectral-norm product bound) — integrated into a CEGIS loop.
threat_model: Norm-bounded, time-varying perturbations added to the controller's observed/next state at each timestep (xt+1 = f(xt,ut) + δt with ||δt||p ≤ δ, evaluated under l∞). These model sim-to-real gaps: modeling errors, actuation/sensor noise, and environmental disturbances. The goal is a certificate whose reach-while-avoid guarantee (reach goal set, never enter unsafe set) provably holds for the entire δ-ball of reachable perturbed next states. Empirically both a worst-case adversary (PGD attacks maximizing the certificate value each step) and uniform random perturbations are considered. Note: the paper's stated scope is perturbations in states only — not perturbations in the dynamics model, observations, or controller output.
limitations: Self-stated: the framework only handles perturbations in states, not broader uncertainty (dynamics-model perturbations, observation-space attacks, or controller-output deviations), which the authors flag as future work for closing sim-to-real gaps. Additional observed limitations: PGD adversarial training does not generalize to perturbations larger than its training radius (a well-known adversarial-training weakness the paper acknowledges, e.g. 2D Docking 0.05 → 17%); evaluation is on only two low-dimensional environments (2D Inverted Pendulum, 4D Docking) with small networks; the descent margin ϵ is chosen heuristically (largest feasible from the vanilla model) and τ via binary search, with τ=0.8 on 2D Docking failing to converge within the 12h timeout; nonlinear dynamics require piecewise-linear over-approximation (OVERT, 3 segments) which introduces conservatism; and certified bounds remain small in absolute terms (e.g., ~0.0084–0.0172).

datasets_used: Inverted Pendulum control benchmark (OpenAI Gym-based; 2D state [θ, θ̇], torque control)
2D Docking spacecraft task (from Ravaioli et al. [29]; 4D state [x, y, vx, vy], thrust control) — safety-critical autonomous-systems benchmark
quantitative_results: Certified bound, Inverted Pendulum: Vanilla 0.0015 → Lip-3 0.0084 (+460%, ~4.6x); Lip-Neighbor 0.0033; PGD 0.0078
Certified bound, 2D Docking: Vanilla 0.0094 → Lip-1 0.0172 (+83%); Lip-Neighbor 0.0156; PGD 0.0131
Certified l∞ volume increase: >30x (2D Inverted Pendulum via Lip-3), >10x (4D Docking via Lip-1)
Empirical success, Inverted Pendulum @0.01 adv: Vanilla 39% vs Lip-3 70%, Lip-Neighbor 69%, PGD 89%; @0.008 adv: Vanilla 41% vs Lip-3/PGD 100%
Empirical success, 2D Docking @0.03 adv: Vanilla 41% vs Lip-1/Lip-Neighbor 100% (2.4x); @0.05 adv: Lip-1 83%, Lip-Neighbor 86%, PGD 17%, Vanilla 34%
5-trial re-run (Appendix E) certified bounds (mean±std), Inverted Pendulum: Vanilla 0.0042±0.0012, Lip-3 0.0072±0.0017, PGD 0.0081±0.0004; 2D Docking: Vanilla 0.0086±0.0009, Lip-1 0.0163±0.0012
Success rate measured over 10,000 sampled initial states, 200-step horizon; ϵ = 1e-2 (Docking), 5e-3 (Pendulum); verifier binary search to interval <1e-4
baselines_compared: Vanilla (standard non-robust CLBF training, satisfying only Eqs. 2-4) — the primary baseline
Internal method variants compared against each other: PGD (adversarial descent loss), Lip-Neighbor (Lipschitz-neighborhood bound loss), Lip-n (global Lipschitz regularization, n = bound τ)
